column2:
Gerald‡Dherbomez Gerald‡"Lien vers les données
** Dataset Overview ** This project contains the dataset collected from the Renault Zoe demonstrator vehicles
column3: part of the PRETIL platform
column4: which belongs to the CRIStAL laboratory at the University of Lille. The data was gathered as part of an experimental study focused on the impact of GNSS spoofing on safety-critical land transportation localization systems within a cooperative fleet.  This dataset was instrumental in conducting the study presented in the paper titled ""Assessing GNSS Spoofing Impact on A Safety-Critical Land Transportation Localization Function Within a Cooperative Fleet: An End-Users Focused Experimental Study"" published in ION GNSS+ 2024.  ** Data context ** The dataset is structured around three Renault Zoe vehicles (referred to as CAR1
column5: CAR2
column6: and CAR3) equipped with various sensors for GNSS
column7: IMU
column8: LiDAR
column9: and CAN bus data. Each vehicle recorded its data using different ROS (Robot Operating System) versions
column10: with CAR1 (White) operating on ROS1 and the remaining two cars (CAR2 Blue and CAR3 Grey) on ROS2. The dataset includes the following sensor data:  - GNSS positions from multiple receivers - 3D point clouds from LIDAR systems - IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit) data from onboard sensors - CAN bus data recording the internal vehicle state (speed
column11: steering
column12:
etc.) - Synchronous timestamps and transforms (TF frames) to enable precise localization and mapping - Spoofed position of CAR1 generated using Safran Skydel GNSS emulator."‡Automotive; Autonomous driving; Lillodata; Multi-vehicle System; Robotics; Spoofing of GNSS signals‡Dataset‡"BlueCar.sbf

"‡Data and statistics of direct numerical simulations of two converging-diverging channel flows‡Laval
column3:
Jean-Philippe‡"Lien vers les données
The dataset includes the raw data and several statistics of two Direct Numerical Simulation (DNS) of converging diverging channel flow (channel flow with a smooth bump profile on the lower wall). The two cases have been designed using the same geometry than the one used in several experiments in the LMFL’s wind tunnel for the analysis of wall turbulence with pressure gradient and for flow control. The current database was generated in order to provide data to test and validate turbulence models. In addition to classical turbulence statistics
column4:
all the terms of the Reynolds Stress budget equation are available in the bump local coordinate system."‡Channel flow; Direct Numerical Simulation; Lillodata; Turbulence‡Dataset‡"APG_400_Iso_Q_3D_Full.avi

column3:
Veronica‡"Lien vers les données

The following Python scripts are used for automatic image classification of radiolarians or more specifically from closely related species of the genus Podocyrtis by using the MobileNet version 1 convolutional neural network (CNN) by Carlsson et al.
column4:
2022.

The first file consists of a script that rotate images into the same angle and resize them into an equal size. The second file consist of a prediction script and the third one a MobileNet version 1 CNN script for training an image dataset.

"‡Impact of a 2-month exercise training program on glycaemic fluctuations on days with and without exercise in adults with type 1 diabetes.‡Heyman
column3:
Léo‡"Lien vers les données
In type 1 diabetes (T1D)
column4: some few studies have explored the impact of training on glycaemic fluctuations. However
column5: no research aimed to assess glycaemic excursions and their progression throughout a 2-month training program
column6:
distinguishing those observed around exercise sessions from those of non-exercising days.
Nineteen (13 female) adults with T1D participated in 2-3 supervised exercise (aerobic/strength) 90-min weekly sessions over 2 months. Glycaemic excursions (continuous glucose monitoring) were calculated for specific periods (24-hour
column7: nocturnal; periods before
column8: during
column9: after exercise sessions) and compared between day with and without an exercise session. Linear mixed models or binary/multinomial logistic regressions with random effects were used to assess the effect of weeks of training and type of day
column10: including or not an exercise session
column11:
on glycaemic excursions. The data made available on data.gouv corresponds to all the data files used for these analyses. Access to the data can also be requested from Elsa Heyman.
During the 2-month training
column12: nights following exercise showed reduced risk of hyperglycaemic (>10.0mmol⋅L-1) compared to non-exercise nights. This difference attenuated with weeks of training
column13: in parallel to a progressive increased risk of time >16.7mmol⋅L-1 during early and late recovery periods after exercise sessions. Overall
column14: independently of exercise session occurrence
column15:
level 1 and 2 nocturnal hypoglycaemic risk increased as the training weeks passed.
In conclusion
column16: for patients with type 1 diabetes who engage in regular physical activity
column17: it is crucial to be vigilant about post-exercise glycaemic excursions
column18: particularly at night. Although acute exercise (i.e.
column19: day with vs. without a training session) reduced nocturnal hyperglycaemia without increasing the risk of hypoglycaemia
column20: this risk of hypoglycaemia progressively increased after several weeks of training
column22: even those not subsequent to an acute exercise. Therefore
column25: using continuous glucose monitoring systems is essential
column26: not only during the first weeks of training
column27: but also after several weeks."‡Continuous Glucose Monitoring; Diabetes Mellitus

The data have been produced as part of the HAL/LO research project on the contributors of the HAL repository. (1) Background: The 2002 Budapest Open Access Initiative recommended on self-archiving of scientific articles in open repositories as the “green road” to open access. Twenty years later
column4: only one part of the researchers deposits their publications in open repositories; moreover
column5: one part of the repositories’ content is not based on self-archived deposits but on mediated nonfaculty contributions. The purpose of the research is to provide more empirical evidence on this situation and to assess the impact on the future of the green road. (2) Methods: We analyzed the contributions on the French national HAL repository from more than 1
column6: 000 laboratories affiliated to the ten most important French research universities
column7: with a focus on 2020
column8: representing 14
column9: 023 contributor accounts and 166
column10: 939 deposits. (3) Results: We identified seven different types of contributor accounts
column11:
including deposits from nonfaculty staff and import flows from other platforms. Mediated nonfaculty contribution accounts for at least 48% of the deposits. We also identified difference between institutions and disciplines. (4) Conclusions: Our empirical results reveal a transformation of open repositories from self-archiving and direct scientific communication towards research information management. Repositories like HAL are somewhere in the middle of the process."‡Lillodata; contributor; institutional repository; open access; open science‡Dataset‡"HAL-contributor-V2-enriched-standard-REQUESTS.txt

column3:
Asif‡"Lien vers les données
The datasets correspond to all data used in the analyses of the paper ""Explainable machine learning to identify activity-related
column4: insulin and dietary thresholds for prevention of exercise-associated hypoglycaemia and hyperglycaemia in free-living children with type 1 diabetes"". All information about the data collection are provided in the paper. Please find below a summary of the study and the results obtained with the data.  Physical activity induces immediate and delayed glycaemic changes in children with type 1 diabetes (T1D)
column5: yet real-life thresholds for exercise characteristics that jointly account for insulin and diet remain poorly defined. In a 7-day free-living study of 36 children with T1D
column6: we combined continuous glucose monitoring with self-reported and accelerometer-derived exercise measures
column7: alongside detailed insulin and dietary data. Ensemble machine-learning models predicted hypoglycaemia (<70 mg·dL⁻¹) and hyperglycaemia (>180 mg·dL⁻¹) during exercise
column8: the 2-hour post-exercise recovery
column9: and the subsequent night
column10: with thresholds identified using Shapley value analysis. The models achieved high predictive accuracy and revealed distinct phase-specific patterns. Pre-exercise glucose levels >175 mg·dL⁻¹ were associated with glucose decline during activity
column11: whereas levels >150 mg·dL⁻¹ predicted glucose increases during early recovery. Insulin boluses exceeding 11% of the total daily dose in the 4 h before exercise and 17% during the 2 h recovery period increased hypoglycaemia risk. Exercise effects were phase dependent: self-reported activity lasting >80 min increased hypoglycaemia risk during exercise
column12: while >15 min of objectively measured vigorous activity reduced risk during early recovery. A pre-sleep glucose range of 100–115 mg·dL⁻¹ minimised nocturnal dysglycaemia
column13: and behavioural patterns such as afternoon exercise and multiple daily sessions were associated with lower overnight hyperglycaemia. Patient characteristics
column14: including age
column15: BMI Z-score
column16: and daily insulin dose
column17: influenced glycaemic risk in phase-specific and sometimes opposing ways. Overall
column18: this analysis identifies quantitative
column19: phase-specific thresholds for exercise and insulin management across the exercise–recovery cycle
column20: moving beyond arbitrary categorisation used in previous studies."‡Child; Diabetes Mellitus

column1: https://doi.org/10.57745/GXQO6F‡https://doi.org/10.57745/GXQO6F‡https://doi.org/10.57745/GXQO6F‡espace-generique://10.57745/GXQO6F‡en‡doi:10.57745/GXQO6F‡2024-09-03T08:21:04Z‡2024-08-27T06:51:21Z‡‡process of information integration in old people with dementia: impact of Physical Activity‡Fruchart
column3:
Eric‡"Lien vers les données
Physical activity enhances cognitive performance and reduces the progression of dementia symptoms (Liu et al.
column4: 2020; Swinnen et al.
column5: 2021; Henskens et al.
column6:
2018). The aim of this research is to investigate the impact of physical activity on the judgment processes of older people with dementia. The aim is to assess the effect of physical activity on their information integration abilities.
Three groups of old people participated in this study. An ""active"" group
column7: consisting of older people with dementia (N=9)
column8: received three ""throwing training"" sessions per week. An ""inactive"" group
column9: also comprising old people with dementia (N=7)
column10: did not engage in any physical activity. Healthy old people (N=13) formed the control group who conducted the assessment only once at T0 (0 months) to obtain baseline results. At 3-month intervals
column11: using a judgment scale
column12: the judgment of success in throwing tasks combining three pieces of information (in cognito) was assessed for the two other groups.  The same scenarios were presented in real-life conditions (in vivo) to the ""active"" group
column13: who performed these throws. Comparative statistical analyses were conducted at T0
column14: T3
column15:
and T6.
The study revealed a difference in judgment processes between old people with dementia and healthy old people. It also demonstrated a difference in judgment processes between old people with dementia who received physical activity and those who did not. Training in real-life throwing scenarios (in vivo) facilitated information integration in the judgment task (in cognito).
Physical practice positively affects judgment processes. It preserved information integration ability in old people with dementia.

column3:
Alberto‡"Lien vers les données
Data sets corresponding to all the figures in the article (main text). This work aims at studying the effect of disorder in a lattice subject to descrete step walks. The lattice is implemented in a double fibre loop set-up. The data sets are produced from oscilloscope traces that record the light intensity extracted from each of the two coupled fibre rings using two photodiodes (Thorlabs DET08CFC) with 5 GHz bandwidth. The oscilloscope time traces are arranged in a series of temporal segments
column4: each one corresponding to the average round trip in the two-fibre loop system. The oscilloscope is a Tektronix MSO64 featuring 6 GHz bandwidth
column5: 10-bit vertical resolution
column7: and a memory record length of 62.5 Mpts corresponding to 2.5 ms. The name of each file corresponds to the specific figure in the main article

column4:
données brutes et analyse statistique issues de l'essai clinique EVAL-PNE*

Introduction
Selon la dernière revue Cochrane
column5: aucune intervention éducative n’est supérieure à une autre dans le management des patients lombalgiques chroniques. La présente étude comparera l’efficacité de l’éducation aux neurosciences de la douleur comparée à une éducation conventionnelle
column7:
chez des patients lombalgiques chroniques.

Méthode
Essai contrôlé et randomisé. Les participants (n=88) ont une lombalgie chronique depuis au moins 1 an. Ils participent à un programme d’école du dos comprenant des interventions éducatives et des interventions thérapeutiques réparties sur 5 jours. Le groupe expérimental reçoit l’éducation aux neurosciences de la douleur. Le groupe contrôle reçoit l’éducation conventionnelle. Les 2 groupes reçoivent les interventions thérapeutiques. Le critère d’évaluation principal est l’incapacité fonctionnelle. Évaluation en pré-intervention
column8: post-intervention
column9:
à j90 et à j365.

Diffusion et éthique
L’approbation éthique a été obtenue par le Comité de protection des personnes d’île de France III. Les résultats seront diffusés par publication et seront présentés lors de conférences scientifiques. Le protocole est enregistré sur clinicaltrial.gov : NCT04179708.

The French Soil Quality Monitoring Network (RMQS) is a national program for the assessment and long-term monitoring of the quality of French soils. This network is based on the monitoring of 2240 sites representative of French soils and their land use. These sites are spread over the whole French territory (metropolitan and overseas) along a systematic square grid of 16 km x 16 km cells. The network covers a broad spectrum of climatic
column3: soil and land-use conditions (croplands
column4: permanent grasslands
column5: woodlands
column7: natural or scarcely anthropogenic land and urban parkland). The physical
column8: chemical and biological properties of the soil are measured on each site
column9: during the first campaign and presently on the second campaign. The spatial and temporal variability of soil properties are explained by biophysical variables
column10: sources of contamination
column11:
history of land-use and management practices on each plot.
The first sampling campaign in metropolitan France took place from 2000 to 2009. This campaign focused on soil contamination assessment and made it possible to map key soil parameters (28 variables) as well as 12 trace metal elements and 70 persistent organic pollutants. The sampling method
column12:
measurements and observations on each site are described in the “RMQS guidelines”1 (in French: “manuel du RMQS” see associated publications). The second campaign started in 2016 and should last 12 years.
The dataset includes raw bulk density and coarse fragment data from samples performed on the pits until 50 cm
column13: according three pseudo-replicates for each corresponding layer. Weight at field
column14: dry weight
column15: coarse fragment weight
column16: volume are available for each pseudo-replicate. Soil moisture

A long-term field experiment was conducted from 1989 to 2007 in northern France in a laomy soil to assess the cumulative effects of cropping systems on soil compaction

column7: crop emergence and yield. Three cropping systems (CSs)
column8: including different crop rotations and cultivations (early or late sowing and harvesting)
column9: were compared. CS I was the succession of spring pea /winter wheat/oilseed rape (flax from 2001)/winter wheat while CSs II and III were the succession of sugar beet/winter wheat/maize/winter wheat. The latter two CSs consisted of different sowing dates
column10: based on two distinct decision rules aimed at minimizing the risk of soil compaction in the CS II or maximizing the duration of the crop in the CS III.  From 2000
column11: a new treatment with superficial tillage (i.e. at 6 cm depth) was introduced into the experiment in order to compare the effects of annual ploughing and reduced tillage on change in soil structure over time. Soil water content was measured for each field operation by taking samples every 0.05 m up to a depth of 0.30 m in the topsoil. Soil compaction and soil structure was evaluated after each sowing using a morphological approach and soil bulk density measurements. The proportion of highly compacted zones
column12: i.e. the zones with a massive structure and no visible macropores in the soil profile
column13: were evaluated with the “profil cultural” method. Dry bulk density was measured with a gamma-ray transmission probe. Seedling emergence rates and crop yield were also measured in relation to cropping systems. This dataset represents an important description of the change in soil compaction level

Jose-Luis‡"Lien vers les données
The French Soil Quality Monitoring Network (RMQS) is a national program for the assessment and long-term monitoring of the quality of French soils. This network is based on the monitoring of 2240 sites representative of French soils and their land use. These sites are spread over the whole French territory (metropolitan and overseas) along a systematic square grid of 16 km x 16 km cells. The network covers a broad spectrum of climatic
column5: soil and land-use conditions (croplands
column7: woodlands
column8: orchards and vineyards
column9:
natural or scarcely anthropogenic land and urban parkland).
The physical
column10: chemical and biological properties of the soil are measured on each site
column11: during the first campaign and presently on the second campaign. The spatial and temporal variability of soil properties are explained by biophysical variables
column12: sources of contamination
column13: history of land-use and management practices on each plot. The first sampling campaign in metropolitan France took place from 2000 to 2009. This campaign focused on soil contamination assessment and made it possible to map key soil parameters (28 variables) as well as 12 trace metal elements and 70 persistent organic pollutants. The sampling method
column14:
measurements and observations on each site are described in the RMQS guidelines. The second campaign started in 2016 and should last 12 years.
The soil tests were carried out on composite samples collected with an auger at two sampling layers: 0-30 cm or cultivated layer named “upper layer” or “layer 1” and 30-50 cm named “subsoil layer” or “layer 2”. Each composite sample was made up of 25 individual sample cores taken on a sampling area of 400 m²
column15: using an unaligned systematic sampling design (see description in the RMQS guidelines). In some cases
column16: a third layer was made up of holorganic layers in forests or meadows
column17: corresponding to pedological horizons OF and OH
column18: when these layers were sufficiently thick
column19:
(at least 1 cm) and continuous over the sampling area.
On each plot
column22: volumetric samples to measure bulk density and coarse fragment content. Usually three pseudo-replicates corresponding to both layer 1 and layer 2 and covering the thickness of each layer were sampled. For holorganic layers
column23: bulk density was calculated from the composite sample
column24:
for which each elementary volume was measured.
The dataset contains the results of bulk density and coarse fragment data from 2168 plots of the first campaign and their available layers and from 2137 plots of the 2nd campaign until 2022. The raw values of bulk density and coarse fragment content are not available here but corrected by layer by applying a weighting taking in account overlapping of samples."‡Bulk density; Coarse fragment; Lillodata; TER Science du sol; monitoring network; soil quality observatory‡Dataset‡"Dataset_A.tab

column1: https://doi.org/10.57745/YGYJSH‡https://doi.org/10.57745/YGYJSH‡https://doi.org/10.57745/YGYJSH‡univ-lille://10.57745/YGYJSH‡en‡doi:10.57745/YGYJSH‡2026-05-11T12:04:32Z‡2026-02-05T21:20:20Z‡‡"Data from the manuscript ""The protective effect of high-intensity interval exercise compared with continuous moderate exercise on glycaemic decline is more pronounced in the postabsorptive vs. postprandial state in adults with type 1 diabetes: a randomised crossover trial"""‡Heyman
column3:
Angéline‡"Lien vers les données
Engaging in physical activity while limiting apprehension related to glycemic disturbances requires a clear understanding of how exercise modality and prandial state influence glycemia in physically active adults living with type 1 diabetes. In this randomized crossover study
column4: twenty physically active adults living with type 1 diabetes completed four experimental conditions (HIIE-PP
column5: CONT-PP
column7: CONT-PA). Exercise was performed on a cycle ergometer and protocols were matched for total duration and mechanical load: both included a 3-min warm-up and a 2-min active recovery at 20% maximal Aerobic Power (MAP); HIIE consisted of 10×1-min intervals at 100% MAP interspersed with 1-min passive recovery
column8: whereas CONT comprised 20 min continuous cycling at 50% MAP. Sessions were conducted either 1.5 h after the start of lunch and the corresponding rapid-acting bolus (postprandial) or 5 h after lunch (postabsorptive). Capillary glucose and blood lactate were assessed at rest
column9: at 10 min of exercise
column10: immediately post-exercise
column11: after 2 min of active recovery and after 10 min of passive recovery.  Heart rate
column12: oxygen consumption (VO₂) and vastus lateralis microvascular blood volume (total haemoglobin [THb]) were monitored throughout exercise. In addition
column13: ratings of perceived exertion (RPE) were recorded every 5 min. Post-session enjoyment was evaluated using PACES. Glycemic excursions were assessed during exercise and up to 10 minutes post-exercise using capillary glucose measurements
column14: and from 30 minutes to 24 hours post-exercise using continuous glucose monitoring (CGM). Carbohydrate (CHO) intake and insulin administration were carefully recorded throughout the post-exercise period.  The results are presented in the paper."‡Exercise; Hypoglycemia; Lillodata; diabetes mellitus

column3:
Timmo‡"Lien vers les données
3D characterization of dislocations is critical for understanding plastic deformation in crystalline materials but remains experimentally challenging. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) provides 3D reconstructions of dislocations but the necessity to use thin-foils
column4: which are difficult to prepare and are small in scale
column5: restricts statistical applicability. Here
column6: we present a proof-of-concept for reconstructing the 3D dislocation microstructure using electron channeling contrast imaging (ECCI) in a scanning electron microscope (SEM). Unlike previous SEM-based approaches relying on serial sectioning
column7: which is time-consuming and remove the material under study
column8: our approach is non-destructive. Two electron channeling contrast micrographs of a single-crystal Ni specimen
column9: acquired at distinct tilt-angles
column10: were used to reconstruct six dislocations via stereomicroscopy. The reconstructed dislocation microstructure reveals glide behavior and instances of cross-slip. This method offers a fast
column11: and non-destructive path to 3D dislocation analyses in bulk materials using standard SEM equipment
column12:
extending tomography concepts beyond TEM to accessible SEM-based workflows."‡Dislocations; Electron channeling contrast imaging (ECCI); Lillodata; Plasticity; Scanning electron microscopy (SEM); Three-dimensional tomography‡Dataset; Image; Text‡"Data deposit.txt

column3:
Eric‡"Lien vers les données
The objective of the study was to compare the ways in which young people
column4: older people
column5: and older people with dementia cognitively integrated three factors (intention
column7: and apology) when making prosecutorial judgments (prosecution and revenge) and theological judgments (resentment and reconciliation). The study used an experimental design. The participants were 34 young people (Mage = 22.12
column9: 36 older people (Mage = 66.03
column11: and 18 older people with dementia (Mage = 75
column12: SD = 10.06). For each moral judgment
column13: the participants were confronted with 12 scenarios built from the combination of the three factors. A set of analyses of variance (ANOVAs) with repeated measures were performed on data. Whatever the judgment
column14: older people with dementia differed from young people and older people with regard to the number of factors considered. Young and older participants used the three information cues (intent
column15: consequence
column16: and apologies) for the four judgment tasks (prosecution
column17: revenge
column18: reconciliation
column19: and resentment) while older people with dementia gave greater weight to intention. In contrast to young and older people
column20:
older people with dementia processed prosecutorial judgments and theological judgments in the same manner. The cognitive impairment does not allow older people with dementia to differentiate moral judgments into two categories. Dementia has a negative influence on process of information integration in moral judgment. This may be useful for clinical practice – caregivers."‡Dementia; Information integration theory; Judgment; Lillodata; Psychology‡Dataset‡"data_theological-prosecutorial-judgments-and-dementia_recherche-data-gouv.tab

column1: https://doi.org/10.57745/XH5HDW‡https://doi.org/10.57745/XH5HDW‡https://doi.org/10.57745/XH5HDW‡univ-lille://10.57745/XH5HDW‡en‡doi:10.57745/XH5HDW‡2025-07-21T08:09:17Z‡2025-07-21T07:52:08Z‡‡Characterization of the physical environment at the municipal level in Northern France‡Paumelle
column3:
Martin‡"Lien vers les données

This dataset was developed as part of a PhD research work titled 'Multi-dimensional description of the environment at the territorial scale : contribution to researching environmental determinants in the etiology of chronic diseases' (Paumelle
column4: 2023). This work also aligns with two research projects: (i) ‘Ecologic historic and environnemental influence in Crohn Disease' funded by the French National Research Agency (CROPS/ANR-20-CE34-0015); and (ii) ‘Health and Environment: from territorial to individual risk' funded by the I-SITE ULNE Foundation (R-PILOTE-01 SANTE et ENVIRONNEMENT project). Finally
column5: this dataset was notably used to develop a territorial typology and define the environmental profiles of municipalities in Northern France. The associated publication
column7:
2023.

The dataset provides an integrated description of the physical and outdoor environment at the municipal level in four French departments: Nord (59)
column8: Pas-de-Calais (62)
column9: Somme (80)
column10: and Seine-Maritime (76). In total
column11: the dataset incorporates 109 spatial indicators characterizing the environmental dimensions of the 3
column12:
041 municipalities within the study area. The administrative boundaries used are based on the 2016 edition of the French National Institute of Geographic and Forest Information (IGN).

The source databases used are exclusively open-access. A total of 23 different databases were used. Regardless of their original format
column13: these datasets were processed to generate spatial indicators at the municipal scale. In the final dataset
column14: three types of indicators can be distinguished: (i) indicators integrated as-is without significant manipulation of raw data; (ii) indicators requiring simple normalization steps (e.g.
column15: scaling by municipal surface area or population); and (iii) indicators calculated through more complex processing. These operations were performed using QGIS (QGIS Development Team
column16: 2021) or the R software (R Core Team
column18:
depending on the complexity.

This dataset provides an integrated description of the physical environment through 109 spatial indicators divided into seven sub-dimensions: (i) contamination levels of environmental media (air
column20: soil); (ii) pollutant emission levels; (iii) proximity to emission sources; (iv) land use; (v) agricultural practices; (vi) territorial naturalness; (vii) climate. These indicators help characterize human activities such as industry
column22: and transport. They also describe the pressures these activities exert on the environment
column29:
environmental amenities are captured through indicators of territorial naturalness.

The objective of this work is to provide insights for reusing open environmental data. The data management steps and protocols developed to generate all spatial indicators are detailed in the second chapter of the thesis manuscript (Paumelle
column30: 2023). This chapter also includes a critical perspective on the dataset (e.g.
column32: interpretative limitations of indicators). For appropriate use of the dataset
column33:
consulting this information beforehand is strongly recommended.

Initially
column34: the dataset was developed to explore the relationship between environment and health
column35: particularly for studying Inflammatory Bowel Diseases (IBD). This explains why the spatial coverage of the dataset coincides with that of the EPIMAD registry
column36: the only French health registry dedicated to IBD <i>(Gower-Rousseau et al.
column38:
this work could be extended geographically since all selected open-access databases are theoretically available nationwide.

To facilitate the adoption and reuse of this dataset
column39: two file formats are provided: (i) a GIS-compatible format titled 'Data - GIS Format.gpkg'; and (ii) a spreadsheet format titled 'Data - Spreadsheet Format.tab'. Additionally
column40: a list of all indicators produced is provided
column41: along with a brief description of each: 'List and Description of Indicators.pdf'. This document is concise
column42:
and it is recommended to refer to Chapter 2 of the thesis manuscript for more detailed information.

column3:
Martin‡"Lien vers les données
This dataset concerns pressure sensor datasets about the spatial evolution of a turbulent wake in the subsonic S620 wind tunnel of ISAE-ENSMA in Poitiers
column4: France. The details about the experimental setup are given in the associated publication.  A porous disk
column5: designed to mimic the wake of a horizontal axis wind turbine
column7: whose wake matches that of a vertical axis wind turbine
column8: were tested in a wind tunnel for Reynolds numbers (based on the generator diameter) in the range of 2.6 x 10^5 to 3.9 x 10^5. Experiments were conducted between 1 and 7 diameters downstream of the disk and from 2 to 12 diameters downstream of the cylinder. The data covers horizontal profiles made for several streamwise distances.  The file ReadMe.txt explains the organisation and how the data can be read using an associated python script. All data concerns pressure sensors

column1: https://doi.org/10.57745/PDZKT6‡https://doi.org/10.57745/PDZKT6‡https://doi.org/10.57745/PDZKT6‡univ-lille://10.57745/PDZKT6‡en‡doi:10.57745/PDZKT6‡2026-03-10T07:09:53Z‡2026-03-04T09:08:44Z‡‡"Data sets of article ""Emergence of Turbulence in a counterflow geometry of 2D Polariton Quantum Fluids"""‡Hainaut
column3:
Clement‡"Lien vers les données
The data deposited in this repository were generated as part of a research project investigating turbulence in quantum fluids of polaritons and are associated with the corresponding scientific publication. They originate from two-dimensional numerical simulations describing the dynamics of a polariton fluid in a semiconductor microcavity. The simulations rely on the numerical solution of a set of coupled Gross–Pitaevskii–type equations describing the excitonic and photonic fields. This model captures the out-of-equilibrium dynamics of polaritons in the coherent regime
column4: including dispersion
column5: excitonic interactions
column7: and coherent optical pumping. In this study
column8: the polariton fluid is driven by two counter-propagating coherent lasers
column9: enabling the exploration of different dynamical regimes of the system. The simulation code used to generate these data is available in open access on GitHub. The archived data primarily consist of two-dimensional complex fields corresponding to the photon and exciton wavefunctions. From these complex fields
column10: the spatial distributions of density and phase for both components of the system can be obtained. From the simulated fields
column11: several physical observables are computed
column12: including the different contributions to the system energy (kinetic energy
column13: interaction energy
column14: etc.)
column15: following the expressions defined in the associated publication. In addition
column16: spatial and temporal averaging of the fields allows the calculation of the first-order autocorrelation function 𝑔(1)
column17: which acts as an order parameter and enables the characterization of the different dynamical regimes of the system.  The name of each file corresponds to the specific figure in the main article
column18: where the specific theoretical and numerical information is given. The files are in H5 format with explicit naming
column19:
allowing a simple reconstruction of the figure. The article will be freely accessible on ArXiv."‡Driven-Dissipative systems; Exciton-polariton; Lillodata; Quantum turbulence; Soliton; Superfluidity‡Dataset‡"fig10.h5

column1: https://doi.org/10.57745/4VDWGF‡https://doi.org/10.57745/4VDWGF‡https://doi.org/10.57745/4VDWGF‡univ-lille://10.57745/4VDWGF‡en‡doi:10.57745/4VDWGF‡2026-02-12T12:39:38Z‡2026-02-09T08:03:25Z‡‡"Data from the paper entitled ""Impact of acute physical activity on cardiac parasympathetic modulation in adults with type 1 diabetes: an observational study controlling for acute glycaemic fluctuations and habitual physical activity"""‡Heyman
column3:
Léo‡"Lien vers les données
In T1D
column4: nocturnal HRV can reflect early autonomic alterations. While habitual physical activity is often linked to better HRV
column5: fewer studies have examined the acute (same-day) effect of MVPA on nocturnal HRV in real-life conditions
column6: while accounting for glycaemic exposure. Adults with T1D without clinical autonomic neuropathy were monitored in daily life using continuous glucose monitoring (CGM)
column7: accelerometery
column8: and overnight RR recordings. Nocturnal HRV was extracted from a 5-minute slow-wave sleep (SWS) segment. Glycaemic exposure was described over an upstream window (wake-up to SWS onset) and a concomitant window (the 5-minute SWS segment). Linear mixed models were used to assess associations between nocturnal HRV outcomes and same-day MVPA
column9: habitual MVPA (mean weekly MVPA)
column10: and glycaemic exposure (see paper for details). The data made available on recherche data gouv correspond to the single dataset used for these analyses. Access to the data can also be requested from Elsa Heyman. In this study
column11: higher habitual activity was associated with higher nocturnal HRV
column12: whereas higher same-day MVPA was associated with lower nocturnal HRV indices
column13: particularly with greater upstream time spent >13.9 mmol.L-1. In conclusion
column14: regular physical activity should be encouraged
column15: and on very active days
column16: strategies to limit time spent in marked hyperglycaemia may help support nocturnal autonomic recovery."‡Cardiac autonomic function; Heart rate variability; Hyperglycemia; Lillodata; Physical activity; diabetes mellitus

column1: https://doi.org/10.57745/VNV5J0‡https://doi.org/10.57745/VNV5J0‡https://doi.org/10.57745/VNV5J0‡univ-lille://10.57745/VNV5J0‡en‡doi:10.57745/VNV5J0‡2025-07-02T13:47:34Z‡2025-04-14T13:22:48Z‡‡16S rRNA sequencing of the gut microbiome of young and aged mice infected with influenza virus at day 0 and at days 4
column3: 14 and 28 post-infection‡Wolowczuk
column4: Isabelle‡Wolowczuk
column5:
Isabelle‡"Lien vers les données

Data correspond to a gut microbiome analysis through 16S rRNA sequencing of caecal samples collected from young and aged mice infected with influenza virus at day 0 and at days 4
column7: 14 and 28 post-infection
column8:
and are included in a publication entitled “Impact of Aging on Gut-Lung-Adipose Tissue Interactions and Lipid Metabolism during Influenza Infection in Mice”.

Influenza remains a major threat to human health
column9: especially for the elderly. Aging leads to substantial changes to lung function
column10: gut microbiota
column11: and white adipose tissue (WAT)—a key endocrine organ regulating energy balance and lipid metabolism. In the current study
column12: we performed a multi-omics analysis to investigate how influenza impacts the gut-lung-adipose tissue axis differently with age. Compared to young-adult mice
column13: aged mice experienced more severe short- and long-term outcomes following infection
column14: along with distinct WAT alterations
column15: including impaired browning
column16: heightened inflammation
column17: and reduced innate immune cell recruitment. Age-related differences were also evident in infection-driven shifts in gut microbiota. Akkermansia levels increased in young mice but not in aged mice
column18: while Faecalibaculum and Muribaculum expanded only in aged mice and were correlated with lung pathology. Serum metabolomics also revealed age-dependent metabolic responses to infection. Compared to their non-infected counterparts
column19: young mice had lower levels of p-Cresol-sulfate and Indoxyl-sulfate alongside higher triglycerides
column20: whereas aged mice showed disrupted glycerophospholipid metabolism. By pinpointing specific gut bacteria as potential probiotics and identifying lipid pathways associated with disease progression
column21: these findings could lead to the development of targeted
column22:
age-specific strategies to mitigate influenza severity in the elderly.

Animals and ethics statement
C57BL/6JRj male mice were purchased from Janvier Labs (Le Genest-Saint-Isle
column23: France). Young-adult mice (2-month-old) and aged mice (18-month-old) had ad libitum access to rodent chow (D12450B
column28: USA) and water throughout experiments. All animal studies were performed in accordance with the ARRIVE guidelines recommendations and the care
column30: and treatment of mice were in agreement with current national and institutional regulations and ethical guidelines (Animal Experimentation and High Technology Platform of the Institut Pasteur de Lille
column31: Agreement # E59-350009). All mouse procedures were approved by the institutional regional ethics committee “Comité d’Ethique en Experimentation Animale” (CEEA)75. Animal studies were authorized by the French Ministry of Education
column32:
Research and Innovation (protocol APAFIS#: 2020013113414570_v2).

Sample collection
After a 7-day acclimatation in the BSL2 facility
column33: mice were anesthetized with ketamine (1.3 mg) (Imalgene1000
column34: Boehringer Ingelheim Animal Health France SCS
column39: Germany) in PBS (100 µL) via intraperitoneal injection
column40: then intranasally inoculated with either a sublethal dose (50 PFUs
column41: diluted in 50 µL PBS) of human-derived
column42: mouse-adapted influenza A/Scotland/20/1974 (H3N2) (infected groups) or PBS (mock-treated groups). Mice were monitored daily for 28 days post-infection (dpi) for signs of morbidity and changes in body weight. At specified sacrifice days (0
column48: mice were euthanized with an intraperitoneal injection of euthasol (40 mg/kg)
column51: adipose tissues (subcutaneous (inguinal) adipose tissue (SCAT) and visceral (epididymal) adipose tissue (VAT))
column52: and caecal contents were collected. Lung and adipose tissue samples were either snap-frozen for gene expression
column54:
or immediately processed for flow cytometry. Sera were stored at -80°C for cytokine and metabolomic analyses. Caecal contents were snap-frozen and stored at -80°C for microbiota analyses using 16S rRNA sequencing method.

16S rRNA gene sequencing
Biomnigene (https://www.biomnigene.fr/fr/
column56: France) conducted the DNA extraction from frozen caecal samples using the E.Z.N.A® Stool DNA kit (Omega Bio-tek
column59: USA). PCR reactions to amplify the V3-V4 hypervariable region of the 16S rRNA gene were performed using the AccuStartTM II PCR SuperMix (VWR International
column62: USA). PCR products were analyzed using a QIAxcel DNA High Resolution Cartridge (QIAGEN). For preparation of libraries
column63: PCR product concentrations were determined using Qubit 4.0
column64: and samples were pooled equimolarly before purification by electrophoresis on PippinHT using a 1.5% agarose cassette (Sage Sciences
column67: USA). Sequencing of V3-V4 amplicons was performed on MiSeq Illumina in 2X251 bp by using the Illumina MiSeq Reagent Kit v2 (500 cycles) (Illumina
column70: USA). Alpha diversity indices (number of observed OTUs and Shannon diversity index) were computed using QIIME v1.9.1. Gene and metabolic pathway abundances were inferred using PICRUSt2 (Phylogenetic Investigation of Communities by Reconstruction of Unobserved States) software (2020)
column71:
based on the 16S rRNA gene sequencing results obtained in QIIME 2.

column3:
Martin‡"Lien vers les données

Environmental profiles of municipalities in metropolitan France – MRC-PE Project

Context

This dataset was developed as part of the MRC-PE research project (Chronic Kidney Disease – Environmental Profiles)
column4:
selected in 2023 through a joint call for projects by the Health Data Hub and the French Ministry of Ecological Transition and Territorial Cohesion (GD4H program).

The scientific objective of the MRC-PE project is to investigate
column5: at the national scale
column6: the association between territorial environmental profiles and the incidence of chronic kidney disease (CKD)
column7: while exploring the potential mediating role of diabetes
column8: hypertension
column9: and other socio-environmental determinants (social disadvantage
column10:
healthcare accessibility).

This multidisciplinary project relies on the linkage of large-scale health data (REIN registry
column11:
SNDS) with a comprehensive set of spatialized environmental indicators.

Dataset content

This dataset provides a detailed description of the outdoor environment at the municipal level across metropolitan France (excluding Corsica).

  • Spatial scale: municipality (n = 34
column13:
administrative boundaries from IGN – 2021 edition)
  • Number of indicators produced: 150 spatialized indicators
  • Data sources: 18 datasets (15 openly accessible; 3 available upon request)
  • Covered themes: (1) Contamination levels of environmental media (air
column14:
soil); (2) Proximity to emission sources; (3) Land use; (4) Agricultural practices; (5) Environmental amenities; (6) Climate.

Detailed processing protocols and R scripts used to build each indicator are available on the following GitHub repository:
https://github.com/LGCgE-SIGLES/MRC-PE_Environmental_profiles

This repository also provides critical information on source data quality
column15: interpretative limitations of the indicators
column16: and general usage recommendations. For appropriate use of the dataset
column17:
it is therefore strongly recommended to consult this additional documentation beforehand.

column1: https://doi.org/10.57745/Z71CGC‡https://doi.org/10.57745/Z71CGC‡https://doi.org/10.57745/Z71CGC‡univ-lille://10.57745/Z71CGC‡en‡doi:10.57745/Z71CGC‡2025-07-02T13:48:56Z‡2025-04-14T14:06:21Z‡‡Serum metabolomics of young and aged mice infected with influenza virus at day 0 and at day 7 post-infection‡Wolowczuk
column3:
Isabelle‡"Lien vers les données

Data correspond to a metabolomic analysis of serum samples collected from young and aged mice infected with influenza virus at day 0 and at day 7 pos-infection
column4:
and are included in a publication entitled “Impact of Aging on Gut-Lung-Adipose Tissue Interactions and Lipid Metabolism during Influenza Infection in Mice”.

Influenza remains a major threat to human health
column5: especially for the elderly. Aging leads to substantial changes to lung function
column7: and white adipose tissue (WAT)—a key endocrine organ regulating energy balance and lipid metabolism. In the current study
column8: we performed a multi-omics analysis to investigate how influenza impacts the gut-lung-adipose tissue axis differently with age. Compared to young-adult mice
column9: aged mice experienced more severe short- and long-term outcomes following infection
column10: along with distinct WAT alterations
column11: including impaired browning
column12: heightened inflammation
column13: and reduced innate immune cell recruitment. Age-related differences were also evident in infection-driven shifts in gut microbiota. Akkermansia levels increased in young mice but not in aged mice
column14: while Faecalibaculum and Muribaculum expanded only in aged mice and were correlated with lung pathology. Serum metabolomics also revealed age-dependent metabolic responses to infection. Compared to their non-infected counterparts
column15: young mice had lower levels of p-Cresol-sulfate and Indoxyl-sulfate alongside higher triglycerides
column16: whereas aged mice showed disrupted glycerophospholipid metabolism. By pinpointing specific gut bacteria as potential probiotics and identifying lipid pathways associated with disease progression
column17: these findings could lead to the development of targeted
column18:
age-specific strategies to mitigate influenza severity in the elderly.

Animals and ethics statement
C57BL/6JRj male mice were purchased from Janvier Labs (Le Genest-Saint-Isle
column19: France). Young-adult mice (2-month-old) and aged mice (18-month-old) had ad libitum access to rodent chow (D12450B
column24: USA) and water throughout experiments. All animal studies were performed in accordance with the ARRIVE guidelines recommendations and the care
column26: and treatment of mice were in agreement with current national and institutional regulations and ethical guidelines (Animal Experimentation and High Technology Platform of the Institut Pasteur de Lille
column27: Agreement # E59-350009). All mouse procedures were approved by the institutional regional ethics committee “Comité d’Ethique en Experimentation Animale” (CEEA)75. Animal studies were authorized by the French Ministry of Education
column28:
Research and Innovation (protocol APAFIS#: 2020013113414570_v2).

Sample collection
After a 7-day acclimatation in the BSL2 facility
column29: mice were anesthetized with ketamine (1.3 mg) (Imalgene1000
column30: Boehringer Ingelheim Animal Health France SCS
column35: Germany) in PBS (100 µL) via intraperitoneal injection
column36: then intranasally inoculated with either a sublethal dose (50 PFUs
column37: diluted in 50 µL PBS) of human-derived
column38: mouse-adapted influenza A/Scotland/20/1974 (H3N2) (infected groups) or PBS (mock-treated groups). Mice were monitored daily for 28 days post-infection (dpi) for signs of morbidity and changes in body weight. At specified sacrifice days (0
column44: mice were euthanized with an intraperitoneal injection of euthasol (40 mg/kg)
column47: adipose tissues (subcutaneous (inguinal) adipose tissue (SCAT) and visceral (epididymal) adipose tissue (VAT))
column48: and caecal contents were collected. Lung and adipose tissue samples were either snap-frozen for gene expression
column50:
or immediately processed for flow cytometry. Sera were stored at -80°C for cytokine and metabolomic analyses. Caecal contents were snap-frozen and stored at -80°C for microbiota analyses using 16S rRNA sequencing method.

Metabolite extraction
Briefly
column51: 10 µL of each serum samples (n=5 young mock
column54: and n=5 aged 7 dpi) were loaded onto a filter containing internal standards for normalization. Filters were dried under a stream of nitrogen and a 5% solution of phenyl-isothiocyanate was added for derivatization. Dried analytes were subsequently extracted with 5 mmol/L ammonium acetate in methanol and analyzed by UPLC-MS/MS. The targeted analysis identified and quantified 1019 metabolites (https://biocrates.com/mxp-quant-500-xl/) detected by MS/MS after UPLC separation and flow injection analysis (FIA) (SCIEX 5500 Triple Quad System
column58: USA). Data were recorded using the Analyst software (SCIEX). For each metabolite
column59:
peaks were quantified using area-under-the-curves. The analytical method has been fully validated by the kit’s manufacturer according to FDA and EMA guidelines.

Metabolomic analysis
To compare metabolite signatures in young and aged mice at baseline (mock-treated) and at 7 dpi
column60: ultra-high pressure liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (UPLC-MS/MS) was used. Sera were processed using the MxP Quant 500 XL kit (BIOCRATES Life Sciences AG
column62: Austria). Metabolites with ≥ 40% missing values or values below the limit of detection (LOD) in all groups were excluded during initial data cleaning. Consequently
column63: only metabolites with ≥ 60% of concentration values above the LOD in at least one of the three experimental groups were included for statistical analysis. Any remaining missing values were imputed using 1/5 of the minimum positive value of each variable. Given the metabolic interactions between adipose tissue and gut microbiota metabolism
column64: the metabolomic analysis was performed using all metabolites retained after data processing
column66:
552 molecules were analyzed.

column4: their impact on soil organic carbon (SOC) stocks remains uncertain. column5: harvest date and irrigation). We analyzed two long-term experiments. Site 1 was located in Estrées-Mons

column9: southern France (43.433°N
column11: and was managed by Arvalis. Both sites were sampled initially and after 12 (site 1) or 13 (site 2) years. SOC concentrations
column12:

"‡Five Radiometric Landscape of Kerguelen Islands‡Lemettais
column2:
Louise‡Lemettais Louise‡"Lien vers les données

Landscape mapping is crucial for monitoring and understanding a wide range of ecological and social processes
column3: as well as for addressing major issues such as land use planning
column4: biodiversity assessment
column5: health-environment      interactions and monitoring the impacts of environmental and resource management policies.      In order to make the landscape approach operational
column6:
it is necessary to have robust methodological frameworks and specific tools for characterising and mapping landscapes at different scales.

In this context
column7: our objective is to develop a map of the major landscapes of the Kerguelen archipelago using      only satellite time series (MODIS SITS). This work is based on the use of the MOD13Q1 product (NDVI
column8: 250 m)      covering the period 2003–2022
column9:
which is used to identify and characterise the main landscape structures.

The dataset provided includes:

  • a methodological diagram describing the steps of the approach adopted;
  • all the data used
column10:
structured around four Essential Landscape Variables (ELVs) derived from the NDVI time series;
  • the final map of the five major types of radiometric landscapes;

    • This approach illustrates the potential of satellite time series for mapping landscapes in sub-Antarctic environments and has resulted in the first radiometric landscape map of the archipelago. It thus provides a valuable reference base for future environmental analyses
column11: such as the study of landscape trajectories
column12: monitoring vegetation dynamics
column13:
and assessing the impacts of climate change.

This work was supported by the Centre National d’Études Spatiales (CNES) through the TOSCA EVOLKER project. Louise Lemettais PhD grant was funded both by university of Lille and CNES.

column4:

The objective of these experiments is to analyze the performances and behaviors of a family of optimization algorithms
column5: we named: 'fractal-based decomposition algorithms'. They hierarchically decompose a continuous search space using a self-similar and self-recurrent geometrical object. These algorithms can be described by five elementary building blocks: fractal
column7: scoring
column8:
exploration and exploitation search components.

To obtain these experimental results
column9: we used 11 different algorithms instantiated with our Python package named 'Zellij'. Each of these algorithms has a unique combination of building blocks. Thus
column10: one can analyze the sensitivity to the five components
column11: dimensionality
column12:
and problem definition.

The summaries are provided as CSV files. Each file contains 10 features (8 for summary_sp500.tab): the dimensionality of the benchmark function

column26:

"‡Data from the paper entitled 'Acute effects of daily life spontaneous and structured physical activity on hypoglycaemia and hyperglycaemia in children with type 1 diabetes: an observational study controlling for carbohydrate and insulin confounders'‡Melin
column3:
Angeline‡"Lien vers les données
In type 1 diabetes
column4: some few studies have attempted to explore the impact of free-living self-reported physical activity (PA) sessions and the impact of accumulated spontaneous PA on hypoglycaemic risk
column5: and less often on hyperglycaemic risk. However
column6:
no research has integrated in their analyses the confounding impact of sedentary time and/or of carbohydrate intake.
Fifty-eight children/adolescents living with type 1 diabetes have worn a continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) and an accelerometer for 7 days of free-living. During these 7 consecutive days
column7: helped by an adult
column8: children/adolescents reported scheduled and unscheduled PA sessions
column9: specifying the exact PA and its time
column10: duration and subjective intensity. For each meal/snack
column11: the participants recorded the exact time
column12: type
column13: amount of food consumed and insulin dose. Linear mixed models or binary/multinomial logistic regressions with random effects were used to assess the effect of free-living PA on glycaemic metrics
column14: controlling for carbohydrate intake
column15: insulin administration and participants characteristics. Two sets of analyses were performed
column16: as described in details in the paper: in the 1st analyses
column17: we explored the effect of self-reported PA sessions on glycaemic metrics during the sessions
column18: during two hours post-sessions and from two hours post-dinner to breakfast the next day. In the 2nd set of analyses
column19: we explored the effect of cumulative PA and sedentary time (recorded with accelerometry) considered over different periods of the day
column20:
on concomitant or subsequent glycaemic (CGM) outcomes. The data made available on recherche.data.gouv correspond to all the data files used for these 1st and 2nd sets of analyses. Access to raw data of CGM and accelerometry would also be possible upon request to Elsa Heyman and Angéline Melin.
Data from 45 children/adolescents (21 females
column23: P<0‧01) reduced day-time risk of >13‧9mmol.L-1 hyperglycaemia. Time spent >10‧0mmol.L-1 during self-reported PA sessions tended to decrease when children consumed less high-glycaemic index carbohydrates during the previous hour (e>+0‧40
column24: P<0‧06) or performed other sessions before the current one (e<−11‧40
column26: the latter conditions did not influence hypoglycaemic risk. The probability of spending time <3‧0mmol.L-1 during the night increased when children performed structured PA sessions of longer duration (OR=1‧02
column27: P<0‧05) as well as when more vigorous-intensity PA was accumulated over the afternoon (OR=1‧07
column28:
P<0‧01).
The detailed results for all analyses are fully described in the paper. In conclusion
column29: increasing spontaneous active behaviours could help to reduce day-time level 2 hyperglycaemia. For children experiencing hyperglycaemia during exercise it could be advised to avoid excessive pre-exercise carbohydrate intake or multiply daily sessions number. However
column30: as only characteristics of PA sessions (and not conditions under which they were undertaken) predicted risk of level 2 hypoglycaemia (nocturnal)
column31: simplified guidelines about hypoglycaemic risk could be developed."‡Lillodata; activities of daily living; diabetes mellitus

The French Soil Quality Monitoring Network (RMQS) is a national program for the assessment and long-term monitoring of the quality of French soils. This network is based on the monitoring of 2240 sites representative of French soils and their land use. These sites are spread over the whole French territory (metropolitan and overseas) along a systematic square grid of 16 km x 16 km cells. The network covers a broad spectrum of climatic
column3: soil and land-use conditions (croplands
column4: permanent grasslands
column5: woodlands
column7: natural or scarcely anthropogenic land and urban parkland). The physical
column8: chemical and biological properties of the soil are measured on each site
column9: during the first campaign and presently on the second campaign. The spatial and temporal variability of soil properties are explained by biophysical variables
column10: sources of contamination
column11:
history of land-use and management practices on each plot. The first sampling campaign in metropolitan France took place from 2000 to 2009. This campaign focused on soil contamination assessment and made it possible to map key soil parameters (28 variables) as well as 12 trace metal elements and 70 persistent organic pollutants.
The second on-going campaign started in 2016 and should last 12 years.
The sampling plot includes a sampling area and a pit at a distance of 5 m.
This dataset includes physical data from plots set from the beginning of the second RMQS campaign. Data come from measurements on dedicated samples from the pit. These measurements are made at Conservatoire Européen des Sols INRAE site d’Orléans. They are : mass of the sample at field
column12: mass of the sample after 105°C drying
column13:
mass of coarse fragments after washing and sieving.
Volumes are measured at field
column14:
according to different methods adapted to field constraints (cylinder method or excavation method).
These measurements allow to calculate bulk density
column15:
soil moisture and coarse fragment content for each sample.
Composite samples were collected with an auger at two sampling layers: 0-30 cm or cultivated layer named “upper layer” or “layer 1”
column16: and 30-50 cm named “subsoil layer” or “layer 2”. Each composite sample was made up of 25 individual sample cores taken on  the sampling area of 400 m²
column17: using an unaligned systematic sampling design. In some cases
column18: a third layer was made up of holorganic layers in forests or meadows
column19: corresponding to pedological horizons OF and OH
column20: when these layers were sufficiently thick
column21: (at least 1 cm) and continuous over the sampling area. This composite sample was used also as a volumetric sample. Samples were distributed along the soil profile to match each composite layer thickness and according to three pseudo-replicates by layer
column22:
in general case.

measurements and observations on each site are described in the “RMQS guidelines” (reference below)."‡Lillodata; TER sciences du sol; bulk density; coarse fragment; monitoring network; soil quality observatory‡Dataset‡"Metadata_raw_data_rmqs2.tab

Capture is made from 4 different locations in the house by 4 sets of Raspberry Pi / CC2531 sniffer. All sub-captures are hour-sliced and may be merged to build bigger ones. Used CC2531 firmware allows RSSI to be available in the IEEE 802.15.4 layer of each frame. This dataset features some attacks in order to assess algorithms aiming at detecting them. A real device association is also present to assess network key exchange security.

column1: https://doi.org/10.57745/ZL4MMK‡https://doi.org/10.57745/ZL4MMK‡https://doi.org/10.57745/ZL4MMK‡inrae://10.57745/ZL4MMK‡en‡doi:10.57745/ZL4MMK‡2023-12-21T09:31:23Z‡2023-12-21T09:28:15Z‡CC-BY 4.0 (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/legalcode)‡Efficiency of vinegar as a cleaning agent of industrial installations contaminated by spores or vegetative cells of Bacillus‡Benezech
column3:
Audrey‡"Lien vers les données
Nowadays
column4: agro-industries are trying to reduce their impact on the environment by reducing waste
column5: natural resources consumption and by entering in a circular bio-economy logic. Cleaning and disinfection
column6: as mandatory for food safety requirements
column7: are considered to be among the most demanding operations in terms of energy
column8: water and chemical with non-negligible impacts on the environment. The FairChain project is a European project aiming to achieve food systems that are more sustainable from an environmental
column9: social and economic perspective. One of the studied cases concerns the potential uses of vinegar produced from pomaces
column10: co-product from apple juice
column11: including cleaning and disinfection. In this study
column12: we investigated whether vinegar could be used during the Cleaning in Place (CIP
column14: 10 min
column15: 3.60 Pa) procedure to inactivate and/or remove Bacillus adherent spores and vegetative cells from stainless steel. We used a commercial vinegar (5
column17: 14 and 20%) and the one produced for the FairChain project (8% equivalent acetic acid). The efficiency of water alone (rinsing) was also tested
column18: to determine the role of the mechanical effect of flow during the CIP procedure.  Coupons were first contaminated with spores of Bacillus subtilis PY79 (hydrophilic) or  of the mutant B. subtilis PY79 ΔspsI (hydrophobic)
column19: subjected to the rinsing and CIP procedures and the amount of residual CFU was estimated. Our results first failed to demonstrate any disinfectant effect of vinegars on both spores. On hydrophilic spores
column21: the effectiveness of CIP was not significantly different to that of water (+/- 3 log reduction)
column22: suggesting a predominant mechanical effect. For the 20% vinegar
column23: an additional effect of 0.2 log reduction was observed
column24: which suggests a slight chemical effect in addition to the mechanical effect on the spore detachment. Concerning the hydrophobic spores
column25: only the most concentrated vinegars (14% and 20%) proved more effective (0.87 and 0.96 log reduction
column26: respectively) than water alone (0.17 log reduction).   Similar experiments were performed on vegetative cells of B. cereus 98/4
column27: a strain isolated from an industrial site. A major disinfectant effect of vinegars was revealed with a 2-log reduction of viable cells by FairChain vinegar
column29: an additional 1-log reduction was observed for the different vinegars
column30: probably resulting from the mechanical effect due to the flow.  We can therefore conclude that 1/ high concentrations of vinegar improved the cleaning of Bacillus spores
column31: 2/ the FairChain vinegar did not promote a significant spore detachment during CIP procedures
column32: 3/ the FairChain vinegar is efficient in disinfecting contaminated surfaces with Bacillus cells We will pursue the study by i) studying the effect of vinegar on hydrophobic spore surface (crust composition and structure) to explain the suspected cleaning effect ii) identifying all the potencies in hygiene of the FairChain vinegar to follow a circular economy logic while using more ecofriendly cleaning produces."‡Lillodata; vinegar

"‡Data from the paper entitled 'Glycaemic effects of a 156-km ultra-trail race in athletes: an observational field study'‡Heyman
column2:
Elsa‡Cassandra Parent‡"Lien vers les données
Objectives
We aimed to explore the impact of an ultra-endurance race on continuously measured glycaemia and to understand potential physiological mechanisms
column3:
as well as the consequences for performance and behavioural alertness.
Methods
Fifty-five athletes (78% men
column4: 43.7 +/- 9.6 years) ran a 156-km ultra-trail race (‘Trail Scientifique de Clécy’: six 26-km laps
column5: total elevation +6000 m). Participants wore a masked continuous glucose monitoring sensor from the day before the race until 10 days post-race. Blood was taken at rest
column6: during refuelling stops after each lap
column7: and after 24-h recovery. Running intensity (% heart rate reserve)
column8: performance (lap times)
column9:
psychological stress and behavioural alertness were explored. Linear mixed models and logistic regressions were carried out.
Results
No higher risk of hypo or hyperglycaemia was observed during the exercise phases of the race (i.e.
column10: excluding stops for scientific measurements and refuelling) compared with resting values. Laps comprising a greater proportion of time spent at maximal aerobic intensity were nevertheless associated with more time >180 mg/dL (P=0.021). A major risk of hyperglycaemia appeared during the 48-h post-race period compared with pre-race (P<0.05)
column11: with 31.9% of the participants spending time >180 mg/dL during recovery vs. 5.5% during resting. Changes in circulating insulin
column12: cortisol and free fatty acids followed profiles comparable with those usually observed during traditional aerobic exercise. However
column13: creatine phosphokinase
column14: and to a lesser extent lactate dehydrogenase
column15:
increased exponentially during the race (P<0.001) and remained high at 24-h post-race (P<0.001; respectively 43.6 and 1.8 times higher vs. resting). Glycaemic metrics did not influence physical performance nor behavioural alertness.
Conclusion
Ultra-endurance athletes were exposed to hyperglycaemia during the 48-h post-race period
column16:
possibly linked to muscle damage and inflammation. Strategies to mitigate muscle damage or subsequent inflammation before or after ultra-trail races could limit recovery hyperglycaemia and hence its related adverse health consequences."‡Glycaemia; Lillodata; Type 1 diabetes; Ultra-trail‡Dataset‡"Behavioral alertness .tab

column3:
Vanessa Nina‡"Lien vers les données

Slovenian Folk Song Ballads

404 Slovenian folk song ballads with family themes
column4:
collected between the years 1819 and 1995.

This dataset is an archive of the “Slovenian Folk Song Ballads” corpus (scores
column5: measure maps
column7: recordings
column8: synchronizations
column9:
metadata). It provides both raw data and data for integration with the Dezrann music web platform: https://www.dezrann.net/explore/slovenian-folk-song-ballads.

Family songs are folk song ballads
column10: i.e.
column11: songs with relatively short but repetitive melodic sections through which the singer narrates a longer story through the lyrics. The melody and lyrics are known not to be fully dependent on each other
column12: meaning
column13: the same melody could be adapted for another lyric and vice versa. Thematically
column14: they fall into the category of those that sing about family destinies
column15: including several motifs from Slovenian
column16: Slavic
column17: and broader European themes. The content often focuses on describing the natural course of family life
column18: from courtship to childbirth. The themes and motifs of family ballads (feudal
column22:
etc.) revolve around socio-legal relations and both immediate and broader family matters in historical periods from which individual ballads originate.

The collection of Slovenian folk song ballads contains transcribed field material collected by Slovenian ethnologists
column24: ethnomusicologists and various colleagues of Glasbenonarodopisni inštitut ZRC SAZU spanning from the years 1819 to 1995. Categorized thematically as family ballads
column26: and includes the initial verse of the lyrics
column31: and song structure (melody and lyric) (see <a href=""https://hal.science/hal-04142315v1/file/2023-ismir-descriptors-pattern-matching.pdf"">(Borsan et al.
column33:
under submission)). 23 of these songs have historical recordings.

and texture annotations

This dataset is an archive of the “Mozart Piano Sonatas” corpus (scores
column9: measure maps
column10: analyses
column11: recordings
column12: synchronizations
column13:
metadata). It provides both raw data and data for integration with the Dezrann music web platform: https://www.dezrann.net/explore/mozart-piano-sonatas.

Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart (1756–1791) was a composer of 18th century Classical style period
column14: recognized as one of the three principal figures of the First Viennese School
column15: alongside Joseph Haydn and Ludwig van Beethoven. He expressed his versatile music ideas in a large palette of genres. His piano sonatas
column16: published over a 15-year period
column17: were composed for various purposes
column18: including educational material and private commissions from aristocrats. The classical sonata (typically for a solo keyboard instrument) is composed of usually three movements
column19:
of which the first generally follows sonata form. Mozart’s sonatas are well known to have a remarkable structural and textural composition.

The corpus consists of complete scores of all 18 sonatas with form
column23: 2 (K280) and 5 (K283) also have texture annotations (<a href=""https://dx.doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.7316712"">Couturier et al.
column24: 2022). Some movements also have synchronized audio. The corpus uses measure maps (Gotham et al.

"‡Photography of the Kerguelen Islands (Field Mission
column3: Louise‡Lemettais
column4:
Louise‡"Lien vers les données

This dataset brings together a series of panoramic and standard photographs collected during the field campaign conducted in the Kerguelen Islands during the austral summer of 2024–2025
column5: as part of the IPEV 136 SUBANTECO program and my doctoral research on vegetation dynamics and sub-Antarctic landscapes. Images were acquired using a panoramic camera (Insta360 X5) and a digital reflex camera (Canon EOS 450D
column6:
18–250 mm IS lens) during pedestrian surveys across eight sites previously identified through satellite-based landscape analyses.

Content of the Dataset

The photographs provide a visual characterization of the five radiometric landscape classes derived from MODIS NDVI time series (2003–2022). For each landscape type
column7: four representative images are included
column8:
illustrating the diversity of habitats and environmental conditions associated with each class.

  • Panoramic and standard images of five radiometric landscape classes
  • Four representative photographs per landscape type
  • File naming includes both the landscape type and geographic area

These data serve both as a field-based validation of satellite analyses and as visual documentation of the spatial organization and heterogeneity of landscapes in the archipelago.

While representative of the areas surveyed during the campaign
column9: this dataset does not cover the full range of      habitat diversity across the Kerguelen Islands. It nevertheless provides a reference sample that can support      research on landscape dynamics
column10: terrestrial biodiversity
column11:
and the impacts of climate change in sub-Antarctic environments.

column2:
Nicolas‡ADENIS Nicolas‡"Lien vers les données

Verbatims (fichiers texte)
column3: analyses verticales du 1er auteur et 2d auteur (fichier texte)
column4:
analyse horizontale de consensus (fichier texte).

BACKGROUND: A clinical trial was recently conducted to compare the efficacy of pain neuroscience education (PNE) with spinal physiology and ergonomics education as part of a short multidisciplinary rehabilitation program (MRP) in patients with persistent low back pain (LBP). A qualitative study could provide additional context for these results.

OBJECTIVE: To explore change in understanding and coping strategies in participants with persistent LBP who received PNE as part of a one-week MRP.

METHODS: Four medical residents conducted semi-structured interviews of 12 patients with persistent LBP and who had participated in a one-week multidisciplinary rehabilitation program including a PNE component. Audio recordings were analyzed thematically by three physiotherapists.

RESULTS: The prespecified themes of understanding and coping were present in the data and analyzed with regard to change. Other themes – other forms of learning
column5: other barriers to change
column6:
satisfaction and criticisms – also emerged. Pain reconceptualization was frequently associated with new adaptive coping strategies. However conceptual change was found in only half of the participants and does not always appear to be sufficient to lead to changes in coping strategies for pain. Other forms of learning (e.g. experiential learning) and non-educational factors (e.g. motivational ones) also seem to influence conceptual and coping change. Satisfactions (e.g. innovative nature of the approach) and criticisms (e.g. lack of interaction and insufficient connection to everyday life) were reported by certain some participants.

CONCLUSION: Strategies that promote conceptual change
column7: safety perception
column8:
and behavioral change are likely to improve the outcomes of PNE within an MRP.

La recherche REAlang a comme objectif
column5: d’évaluer la performance des classes du CE2 (8-9 ans) à la fin de la scolarité obligatoire (14-15 ans) concernant des savoirs grammaticaux et orthographiques fondamentaux
column6: autour du verbe et de l’adjectif. L’objectif général de REAlang est de rendre compte des réalités d’enseignement-apprentissage de la langue en milieu scolaire. L’enjeu méthodologique sur lequel nous allons précisément revenir ici est la conception et l’utilisation de différentes variables de rendement (performances d’élèves dans un domaine défini) dans le but de cerner au mieux les classes et les niveaux et donc
column7: potentiellement
column8:
de mieux comprendre les pratiques des enseignants.

Ce préalable posé
column9:
un cadre méthodologique structurant le projet de recherche REAlang a pu être élaboré :
  • s’appuyer sur des données quantitatives solides ;
  • proposer des tests duplicables et d’usage facile dans les classes (consignes précises
column10: accessibles facilement aux enseignant.e.s et aux élèves
column11:
non chronophages) ;
  • organiser deux passations au sein des classes impliquées
column12:
une en début d’année scolaire (septembre-octobre) et une en milieu (janvier pour REAlang) ou fin d’année scolaire (avril-mai pour REAlang²).

Ce dispositif a fourni un ensemble de données et d’éléments transférables ensuite pour pouvoir comparer ou élaborer des recherches plus ciblées sur des classes
column13: des suivis d’élèves
column14:
des utilisations en formation.

Une phase de recueil a eu lieu en 2022-2023
column15: appelée REAlang²
column16:
pour laquelle la méthodologie a été revue et le volume du corpus augmenté. Ce sont ces données REAlang2 que présente ce dépôt.

The dataset was produced for a scientometric study on French PhD theses in the field of sustainable development
column4: presented at the Twenty-Fifth International Conference on Grey Literature ""Confronting Climate Change with Trusted Grey Resources""

We reused a dataset with metadata of 431
column11:
997 theses (""Thèses soutenues en France depuis 1985"") produced by ABES and available as public open data at the following address: https://www.data.gouv.fr/fr/datasets/theses-soutenues-en-france-depuis-1985/

The ABES dataset was downloaded in csv format on August 16
column12: 2023. Our dataset is a subsample of the ABES dataset; it contains 3
column13:
467 theses which represent 0.8% of the total number of theses in the ABES file.

The purpose of the study is to assess the French PhD theses on sustainable development
column14: with two objectives: to give a scientometric overview on the French PhD landscape in the field of sustainable development; and to show how PhD theses (as a major part of grey literature) and related tools can be helpful for the scientometric study of science. The review is based on data from the French national portal theses.fr. The results of our study provide a detailed review of the French PhD research on sustainable development from 1985 to 2022
column15: including the main French research universities in the field of sustainable development and the most eminent academic scholars
column16: the disciplinary distribution of the research on sustainable development
column17:
and the accessibility of the PhD theses on sustainable development (open science). The analysis of the year of defense will allow a longitudinal approach to these aspects.

column3:
Nessim‡"Lien vers les données
MassiveFold data generated for CASP16
column4:
in collaboration with CAPRI .
8040 predictions were generated for the target = 1005 x 8 sets of parameters.
- a description of the setup can be found in `MassiveFold_CASP16_Abstract.pdf`
- `README.txt` describes the contents of the main `MassiveFold.tar.gz` file
- the main `MassiveFold.tar.gz file contains all the predictions
column5: divided into 8 folders named after the conditions. It contains predictions as well as pickle files
column7:
rankings and plots. The README.txt file describes the contents of this tar.gz.
- the `only_pdbs_MassiveFold.tar.gz` is the result of the `gather_runs.py` script
column8:
without the pickle files. It contains a list of all the pdb files and ranking files with scores.
- `gather_runs.py` allows to gather the runs
column9: to use preferentially to the one included in the main `MassiveFold.tar.gz` at the time of the prediction phase
column10:
because it has been updated
- `combined_scores.csv` file contains the CASP assessment for the target (from https://predictioncenter.org/)
- `CAPRI_assessment.csv` file contains the CAPRI assessment of MassiveFold predictions for the target. The `chains` column that gives the chains involved in the interface was filled a posteriori from CAPRI generated files and is provided without any warranty.
- `CAPRI_scoring.pdb.gz` is a multi-pdbs containing all the MassiveFold predictions for the target. It was used for the CAPRI scoring phase.
